zotero.abstractNote: Differential privacy (DP) is the gold standard for privacy-preserving data analysis. However, the utility of DP mechanisms often degrades significantly when the data is high-dimensional or complex. A common strategy to mitigate this is to perform non-private pre-processing on the data before applying a DP mechanism. While this approach is widely used in practice, its privacy implications are often poorly understood. In this work, we provide a theoretical framework to analyze the privacy guarantees of DP mechanisms when applied to non-privately pre-processed data. We show that if the pre-processing function satisfies certain stability properties, the composition of the pre-processing and the DP mechanism can still provide strong privacy guarantees. We demonstrate the utility of our framework by applying it to several common pre-processing techniques, including dimensionality reduction and feature selection, and show that it leads to improved privacy-utility trade-offs in practice.
